category_title: Minimally verbal young children with severe ASD and language processing deficits
minimal_context: The student and therapist are sitting one-on-one using picture cards to practice imitating the vowel sounds /a/, /i/, and /u/.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Here is a picture card with a drawing. Say /a/.

STUDENT: (Does not sustain eye contact with the therapist and reaches for the bubbles on the table, making no sound.)

TUTOR: Look here. Try to say /a/.

STUDENT: (Engages in exploratory play by tapping the picture card, showing minimal communicative intent and producing no meaningful vocalizations.)

TUTOR: Let's look at this next drawing. Say /i/.

STUDENT: (Shows a reduced response, failing to participate in joint attention or imitate the adult's model, and does not produce any meaningful words.)

options.A: Reposition the student so she is less distracted by the bubbles on the table, ensuring she is not seeing other items during the vocal imitation activity.
options.B: Shift the focus of the session to a progressive and methodical tuning of awareness to each body part, guiding the student through a body scan during the activity.
options.C: Provide the model and prompt the student to imitate the adult's model using pictorial cues composed of picture cards with drawings of the targeted vocal shapes and oromotor expressions.
options.D: Include increased opportunities for the student to exercise control during the session (e.g., select the picture cards, choose the bubbles, or determine the order of the vowel sounds).
